1: Aramaic continues (Ezra 5:1–Ezra 6:18). hitnaббî — hithpael of nābāʾ (H5013 Aramaic; = Hebrew H5012 nāḇāʾ), 'prophesied.' Haggai and Zechariah are named explicitly — their canonical books (Haggai and Zechariah 1–8) cover precisely this period (520–518 BCE). The prophetic word is the direct catalyst for resuming what the imperial letter had stopped. Divine speech overrides imperial decree. bəšum ʾĕlāh yiśrāʾēl — 'in the name of the God of Israel' — the divine authority grounding the resumption.
2: qāmû — 'rose up' (Aramaic qûm, H6966); the prophetic word produces immediate action. yadaʾ ʾĕlāhāhôm ʿălêhôm — 'the hand of God was with them' (Aramaic yaḏ). 'The hand of God' (yad ʾĕlōhîm) is a standard Hebrew/Aramaic phrase for divine enablement — it recurs throughout Ezra in both languages (Ezra 4:7; Ezra 7:6,9,28; Ezra 8:18,22,31). The prophets provide the word; the hand provides the power; the leaders provide the obedience.
3: Tattenai — governor of Trans-Euphrates, attested in a cuneiform tablet from 502 BCE as 'Tattennu governor of Across-the-River' (Tattenai tablet, Oriental Institute), confirming the historicity of the name. man-śām lāḵôm ṭəʿēm — 'who issued you this decree?' (ṭəʿēm again, the operative word for official authorization). The challenge is procedural: the officials need to verify that the building has proper imperial authorization before allowing it.
4: māh-šəmahaṯ gaḇrayyāʾ ḏî binyānāʾ ḏənāh bānyîn — 'what are the names of the men building this building?' The question is a census/registry request — standard administrative procedure for accountability. The Aramaic gəḇar (H1400) = man/person; šəm (H8036) = name. The names would be needed to make formal charges if the building was deemed unauthorized.
5: ʿên ʾĕlāhāhôm — 'the eye of their God was upon the elders.' Aramaic ʿayn (H5870), identical to Hebrew ʿayin. The 'eye of the LORD' metaphor for divine protection and surveillance recurs throughout Scripture (Psalms 34:15; 2 Chronicles 16:9). Its placement here is theologically precise: the eye of God suspends the normal consequence of the officials' challenge — they 'could not stop them' (lit. 'they did not make them stop') until Darius's word arrived. Divine watchfulness is the reason the building continues through the administrative delay.
6: The letter heading follows the same Aramaic epistolary convention as Ezra 4. ʾăpārəsəkāyēʾ — the associates/inspectors; the same group as in Ezra 4:9,17. The letter is addressed 'to King Darius' rather than 'King Artaxerxes' — the narrative has moved chronologically back to Darius I (521–486 BCE), the Persian king who will ultimately authorize the building (Ezra 6).
7: šəlāmā kullāh — 'all/complete peace' (Aramaic kōl šəlām). Imperial Aramaic letters consistently open with this formulaic greeting. The inclusion of 'all' (kōl/kullāh) is a superlative intensifier — the greeting is perfunctory but follows proper form, signaling the letter is legitimate official correspondence, not a hostile accusation.
8: lêbêt ʾĕlāhāʾ raḇbāʾ — 'the house of the great God.' The Persian governor Tattenai calls the Jerusalem temple the house of 'the great God' (ʾĕlāh raḇ) — the same adjective applied to Persian and Babylonian deities. This is not a confession of faith but an administrative reference; Persian religious policy was to acknowledge the gods of subject peoples. aḇnê gəlāl — 'dressed/hewn stone' (gāll, H1541 Aramaic, = rolling/large stones). The building description matches 6:4 specifications.
9: šəʾêlnāʾ … man-śām lāḵôm ṭəʿēm — the same authorization question from v3 is now relayed verbatim in the letter. The repetition (Wiederholung technique) is characteristic of Hebrew/Aramaic prose — direct speech and reported speech match word-for-word, preserving the precision of the original exchange for the royal record.
10: Names are requested for the record — standard administrative accountability. The Aramaic infinitive ləhôḏāʿûtāḵ = 'to make known to you' (haphel of ydaʿ, H3046); the haphel is the Aramaic causative corresponding to Hebrew hiphil. The bureaucratic thoroughness of the Persian system is evident: names, authorization, and activity all need documenting before the king can adjudicate.
11: ʾănaḥnâ himmô ʿaḇdôhî ḏî-ʾĕlāh šəmayyāʾ wʾarqāʾ — 'We are servants of the God of heaven and earth.' The elders' self-identification is theologically striking: they describe YHWH as 'God of heaven and earth' (ʾĕlāh šəmayyāʾ wʾarqāʾ) — the maximal title affirming universal sovereignty. This title echoes Genesis 14:19 (El Elyon, creator of heaven and earth) and is used in Ezra precisely because it claims YHWH's jurisdiction exceeds any empire's. The historical claim that follows (Solomon built this house long ago) asserts prior right.
12: ḥanăpū ʾăḇāhăṯānāʾ lêʾĕlāh šəmayyāʾ — 'our ancestors provoked the God of heaven to anger.' Aramaic ḥanăp (H2610) = to pollute/profane; the Aramaic root differs from the Hebrew kāʿas (provoke to anger) but achieves the same effect. The elders in their reply to Tattenai include a theological explanation of the exile — it was not divine weakness but divine judgment (yəhab himmô bəyaḏ Nəḇûḵaḏneṣṣar, 'he handed them over to Nebuchadnezzar'). The passive causative acknowledges YHWH's agency in the exile, which paradoxically strengthens their claim: the God who judged them is also the God now enabling the restoration.
13: bišnaṯ ḥăḏāh — 'in the first year' (Aramaic ordinal construction). Cyrus of Babylon (= Cyrus II, who conquered Babylon in 539 BCE) issues the decree in his first year as king. The elders cite Cyrus's authorization as the legal basis for building. ṭəʿēm śām — 'issued a decree' — the same ṭəʿēm root that the adversaries challenged in v3 and v9; the elders now produce the authorizing ṭəʿēm.
14: ʾăniyāʾ ḏîḏəhab wəḏî-ḵəsap — 'vessels of gold and silver.' The temple vessels are tracked from Nebuchadnezzar's looting (v14) through Cyrus's restoration order (v15) — their movement is a subplot of the entire Ezra narrative (cf. Ezra 1:7-11). The vessels function as material proof of covenant continuity: what was taken in judgment is returned in restoration. Šəšbaṣṣar (= Sheshbazzar) receives them as governor (pəḥāh, H6347).
15: šqal ʾillēn ʾĕzal-yəhūb bəhêḵāl bîrûšlem — 'Take these and go, deposit them in the temple in Jerusalem.' The three Aramaic imperatives (take, go, deposit) express Cyrus's direct command. wəḇêṯ ʾĕlāhāʾ yitbənēʾ — 'let the house of God be rebuilt' — passive jussive, appropriate for an imperial authorization that creates permission rather than commanding personal action.
16: yəhab ʾušśāʾ ḏî-bêt ʾĕlāhāʾ bîrûšlem — 'laid the foundation of God's house in Jerusalem.' Sheshbazzar laid the foundation under Cyrus; Zerubbabel (under Darius) resumed the superstructure. Two separate foundation-laying moments (Ezra 3:10 under Zerubbabel; here under Sheshbazzar) likely reflect two distinct phases of the early restoration. The phrase ʾūšşāʾ (Aramaic ʾūššāʾ, H787) = foundation/base; the foundation is the theological symbol of covenant recommitment.
17: wəkhət yiṭṭab ʿal-malkāʾ — 'if it pleases the king' (lit. 'if it is good upon the king') — standard Aramaic petition formula for requesting a royal search. The elders' strategy is brilliant: rather than demanding rights, they request a search of the king's own archives. The Persians prided themselves on accurate record-keeping; appealing to the archives shifts the burden of proof to the imperial record itself. dāḵrônāyāʾ (H1799 = records/chronicles) recurs from Ezra 4:15 — the same archives the adversaries cited are now invoked to vindicate the builders.
